title: Last Man Standing
original_prose:
“Cut your teeth on the lack of answersAnd scream your name into the voidIf no one’s there to hear it happenDo you even make a sound?”

— Sturgill Simpson, Last Man Standing, Sound & Fury



####

“The Wall is our covenant. The Towers are our womb.Through purity and pain, the future is born.”

— SynRgy Birth Doctrine, Article VI



####

I didn’t remember passing through the wall.

One moment, the Strip shimmered ahead like a mirage—glass and chrome lit from within by suffering. The next, I was inside it.

Even now, twenty years after I first saw it, it still awed me.

SynRgy One was nothing like the world I’d grown up in. It was clean. Controlled. Safe. The air smelled of citrus and bleach. Every building hummed. Every street was scrubbed to the bones. Every smile was programmed.

I walked past fountains rimmed in white tile, their basins dry and cracked. A sign posted beside one read:WATER IS SACRED. THANK YOU FOR YOUR SACRIFICE.

Pilgrims still came—still knelt outside the gate, hoping. Some screamed the hymns. Some wept. Some had gone silent long ago.

Above them, the black towers ringed the city like monoliths. One every quarter mile. Hundred-story vertical cities. The breeding programs lived there—artificial wombs, captive mothers, rows of tested children.

They weren’t hidden. They were the skyline.

And I knew why. Any army that tried to breach the walls would have to kill the babies first. Voss didn’t just defend the city with steel. He defended it with guilt.

A drone passed overhead. It scanned me, beeped, and moved on.

The light from the Strip wrapped everything in gold. Children laughed. Holograms of Cindy Sheer spun slow in the air, arms open in eternal welcome. Minorities swept the walkways and power-washed the walls. Their uniforms bore the same stitched phrase:SERVICE IS ASCENSION.

I didn’t stop. I kept walking.

And still, all I could think about was Kat.

The deeper I moved into the city, the more the lines between heaven and hell blurred.

There were three kinds of people here.

The first were the residents—all technically employees of SynRgy. Clean, white-clad, always smiling. Their children wore matching uniforms and played in scripted joy. Their skin was mostly pale. Their teeth were straight. They never looked too long at the towers.

The second were the workers—brown and Black bodies in blue and gray uniforms. Maintenance. Sanitation. Child transport. Security. Most had no Spark. Their names were numbers. Some had been born inside the system, others harvested from crumbled cities across the continent. They spoke little, kept their heads down. The ones who looked up too often didn’t last long.

And then there were the pilgrims. The refugees. The desperate. Most waited outside the walls, but some had been granted access for processing. They wandered the lower tiers of the city like ghosts—draped in old clothes, clutching laminated prayer cards, tracing symbols from the Book of Voss on the ground with trembling fingers.

Signs were everywhere:INSTANT ASCENSION AVAILABLE. NO RESTRICTIONS.

I knew what that meant.

You couldn’t power a city like this—not just its lights and fountains, but the largest server farm in recorded history, buried deep beneath the Stratosphere—on hopes and promises.

It took Spark.

It took souls.

The Core beneath Voss’s throne drank them by the millions. Quietly. Efficiently. Without waste.

I passed a meat shop window—not butchered animal, but processed human cuts. Each slab labeled not just by grade, but by ethnicity. Racks of neatly shrink-wrapped flesh in hues of deep brown, yellow-olive, pink. Labels in gold font:HISPANIC — 12 DAY DRY-AGEDBLACK — LOUISIANA CUTCAUCASIAN — LOW-FATASIAN — SWEET MARINADE

A mother stood with her young son, gazing into the display. She wore a SynRgy armband and the air of someone who’d long stopped questioning the menu.

“I was thinking Mexican tonight,” she said. “From the South. They say it’s fattier, but sweeter.”

The boy squinted at the trays. “I can’t tell the difference in any of them,” he said, too honest.

The father, behind them, laughed. “You’ll learn. Trust me—you can taste the soil in southern cuts.”

“Isn’t it still radioactive down there?” the mother asked.

The father shrugged. “Maybe. Doesn’t matter. When I go, I’ll ascend. Heaven High doesn’t care what’s in your blood.”

I didn’t break stride. But I remembered the taste of camp protein. I remembered wondering what it used to be called. And I knew the father was right. Consume enough of it, and you could tell things about it.

I tugged at my collar. The heat was sharp, dry, relentless. It had once been worse—on the Gulf Coast, the humidity had turned men into puddles. But this… this felt like sterilization. The heat didn’t cling here. It seared and vanished. Like it wanted to forget you.

Everyone pretended not to notice it. Like pain was impolite.

A screen above me lit up. Cindy Sheer smiled, her voice honey-warm:Let your suffering be sanctified. Let your Spark serve the world.

I walked on.

Then I heard it—faint, warped, like wind through a rusted gate. Screaming. Not one scream, but millions. Layered. Wrong.

To my left, a man in a tattered SynRgy tunic sat cross-legged on the curb, grinning like he'd been blessed. The device in his lap looked like a phone, but only barely—its back was torn open, parts exposed, wires soldered to a solar panel, zip ties and antenna coiled like bone spurs off the casing.

"The signal’s so strong here," the man said, eyes shining. "I can hear them all. Every one of them. I think... I think my daughter’s in there. They harvested her back in Oklahoma. She should be here, right?"

I stopped. Only for a second.

The man held the thing to his ear.

His smile collapsed, replaced by something darker, something knowing.

It didn’t drop from the sky. It didn’t make a sound. One blink, and it was there—standing in the shadow of a pillar that hadn’t been casting one.

The WRAITH was so still it hurt to look at. A human shape stretched too long, too thin, matte black from head to toe. Its helmet shimmered like obsidian, reflecting nothing. Not even light.

The man never saw it.

The WRAITH reached out, fingers like razors, and touched the man’s forehead. A blue arc danced. His body convulsed once, twice. Then froze.

A quiet hum filled the air—low, deep, harmonic. The man’s skin darkened, then flaked. His veins lit from the inside. Steam rose from his eyes and mouth. He didn’t scream anymore.

His body aged in seconds. The skin wilted, sagged. His cheeks collapsed inward as if some internal scaffolding had been stolen. Liver spots bloomed across his face, then blistered. His hands shriveled to claws. His spine curled. Every inch of him withered like paper set too close to flame.

His eyes remained open long after the rest of him had given up, wide and watering and ancient, until even they dried and cracked. One final breath hissed out like air from a deflated lung.

By the time he crumbled, he looked ninety. Maybe a hundred.

The WRAITH retracted its hand. Tilted its head.

And vanished.

I had seen them work before. Hell, I’d commanded squads of them. But it always unsettled me. This one in particular.

Like it hadn’t enforced a law.

Like it had fed.

No sooner had the WRAITH disappeared than another vehicle rolled up—quiet, unhurried, painted in white and mint like an old ice cream truck. It hummed softly, bearing a cartoon rabbit and the wordsPROTEIN RECLAMATION UNIT 12stenciled across the side.

Two men stepped out. Both Black. Both wearing spotless pastel uniforms with little bowties and gleaming shoes—like something from a racist’s idea of a white utopian 1950s where slavery never ended. Like they’d been plucked from a theme park version of obedience.

They said nothing.

One opened the back hatch. The other grabbed what was left of the body—a loose husk barely held together—and tossed it in with four others. The truck didn’t shudder. It didn’t care.

Then they sterilized the curb. Foam. Scrub. Steam.

I watched.

I knew where the truck was headed. Out past the towers, near the waterlines. To the reclamation plant. Where everything was broken down. Where the last of the dead would be rendered clean.

Where meat became sacred again.

Behind me, a child clapped once. Too loud. Too happy.

A tourist turned to take a photo. The WRAITH had already vanished.

One of the reclamation workers crossed himself and muttered, "Thank you for your contribution to the Greater Flame."

Another shook his head and said under his breath, "They scream longer the more they mattered."

I didn’t react. I didn’t slow. But my jaw set.

I kept walking.

Listening to the Core was a capital offense. Not because it was dangerous—but because it was true.

Inside that network of servers and Spark converters, every soul screamed. Not collectively. Individually. Alone. Forever.

The body was gone. The mind was preserved, stripped, burned into circuit logic. The result wasn’t unity. It was solitary damnation—no senses, no voice, no time. Just the endless, unknowable scream of the self.

People listened hoping for closure. For connection. For mercy.

All they ever heard was,Let me out.



####

I entered the Stratosphere checkpoint alone. My orders were logged. I was expected.

The tower rose higher than anything I’d seen west of the Mississippi. Like a needle jabbed into the earth. Defensive turrets lined its perimeter. Surveillance drapes flickered between camouflage and warning red. The foundation had been sealed in glassrete, surrounded by a trench that dropped into shadow.

They’d tunneled deep for the Core. Beneath the throne. Beneath the temple. Maybe even through the mantle. Rumor said you could feel the damn thing humming under your feet. Some claimed it wasn’t a hum. It was a whisper.

We weren’t supposed to talk about it.

I passed two facial scans, a retinal reader, and a Spark verification checkpoint. The guards didn’t speak. Everyone in the lobby was white. The air was too clean. Too dry. Recycled until it didn’t feel like air anymore. Even the silence felt manufactured.

An elevator waited for me. The doors whispered shut.

Level 5. Operations Command.

It climbed in silence. Too much silence. Level 3 flashed red. Level 4 reeked faintly of bleach. Level 6 was locked. My ears popped, not from pressure—from presence.

By the time the doors slid open, my palms were wet.

My CO sat at a minimalist desk, wrapped in panels full of deployment maps, desalination stats, Core yield metrics. He didn’t stand.

“You brought in the heretic,” he said flatly. “Good.”

I said nothing.

“Twelve WRAITHs lost. You know what that costs?”

“Yes sir,” I said. I braced. Waited.

He smiled. Thin. Tight. “Someone who loses twelve WRAITHs in one day usually earns a one-way trip to the harvest.”

I nodded. I’d calculated the risk. I didn’t regret it. I saw her again. I should have never brought her here. What was I thinking?

“I understand, sir.”

I waited for the WRAITH. Waited for the floor to open. For the world to end.

But he leaned back.

“You’re lucky, Lieutenant. The brass want optics. You’ll be paraded at the prisoner’s execution. Public hero. Dress blues. Smile for the cameras.”

Relief didn’t reach my chest, but it loosened something in my throat.

“Has the prisoner provided any intel? Anything to help end the war?” I asked.

He laughed. Sharp. Nasal. “Son, the war’s done. You just fought one of the last actions. The rebels are scattered. Oh, there are rats chewing on Denver, but we remind them weekly why that’s a mistake.”

He turned a panel. Live satellite view. High plains. Burning softly under night vision.

“Command’s pivoting,” he said. “From domestic enforcement to global reclamation. And you? You brought in the last rebel commander. You’re a symbol now. More WRAITHs. More reach. You’ll carry Voss’s will to the world.”

He leaned forward.

“This is a good day. For SynRgy. For you. For our Lord Voss.”

I didn’t answer. Just nodded again.

He tapped the desk. “They’re even willing to overlook other indiscretions. For now. Optics and all that.”

Heat rose behind my ears. I wondered if they saw it in the footage. In my voice. Or if they heard it straight from my thoughts. Or worse—if they felt it.

“I understand, sir. If I may—when is she scheduled for termination?”

“She?”

“Yes sir. The prisoner.”

His smile curled again. Oil-slick.

“End of the week, son. Be there. Full dress. You’ll get details soon.”

I stood to go.

“And son,” he added, “you may get the chance to meet Voss. Not many do. So appreciate this moment. And his mercy.”

“Yes sir.”

He flicked his hand.

“Dismissed.”



####

The void is quieter now.

Not silent—just waiting. Like it exhaled and isn’t sure it wants to breathe back in.

I sit cross-legged in the dark, sword across my lap. No sound. No breath. Just weight. The kind of stillness that remembers war like a smell that won’t come out of your skin.

The Child watches me. Not curious. Just… present. Like it’s always been here.

“So you did good,” it says.

I don’t respond right away. I run my thumb along the edge of the blade—slow, careful. Not to test it. Just to feel. The metal’s too perfect. Too clean. No nicks. No heat. No proof that it ever mattered.

My thumb slips. A thin red line opens up.

“You were a hero. Right?”

I stare at the blood. It doesn’t fall. Just pools.

“Sure,” I say. “I guess so.”

The Child tilts its head. “You didn’t feel like one.”

“No,” I say. “Didn’t.”

The sword doesn’t hum. Doesn’t vibrate. But it feels like it’s watching me.

I wipe the blood on my pants. The smear is the only color left in the void.

“They gave you medals.”

“Yeah.”

“Said you’d meet their god.”

“Yeah.”

“And all you could think about was her.”

I don’t answer.

“So is that when it started?” it asks. “When you stopped obeying?”

I look up. Its eyes aren’t judging. Just open. Like they’re holding space for the truth.

“No,” I say. “It started long before that. But it’s the first time I didn’t lie about it.”

The Child nods. Not copying me. Just accepting.

“And the pain? That cut—does it hurt?”

“A little,” I say. “Not enough.”

“Will you keep going?”

I nod.

“Even though they’re watching you again?”

“Especially because of that.”

The Child blinks. Once. Twice.

“You smiled just now.”

I hadn’t noticed. But yeah—I can feel it now. Just a twitch. A curl at the corner. Not joy. Not peace.

Just readiness.

“Yeah,” I say. “Guess I did.”

It leans in, voice small and real.

“Then go.”

So I do.



####

The walk to the tower felt longer than it should’ve. Like time was stretching to punish me.

I don’t know what I thought would happen. That I’d bring her back here, tell her she was wrong, and somehow we’d both get clean? That I’d save her by putting her in a cage? That the cage wouldn’t kill her?

I was a fucking idiot.

Tower 13 loomed ahead—no sign, no door, just seams in the obsidian glass where the walls folded inward. You only noticed the entrance if you were meant to.

Two guards snapped to attention as I approached. One saluted, fist across his chest. The other grinned.

“Ghost of Galveston,” he said, like it was a blessing.

I didn’t answer. Just walked past them.

Inside, the air changed. Thicker. Recycled in a way that made your tongue dry. The lights were soft, antiseptic. Clean in that way nothing alive should be.

The first hallway was lined with holoscreens—loops of my last operation playing on repeat. Kat dropping to her knees. My sword lowering. Her hands rising. The signal distortion cleaned and scrubbed until it looked like surrender.

A group of younger cadets passed me on their way out. One stopped, stared.

“Sir,” he said, eyes wide, “it’s an honor.”

I nodded. That was all I could give.

The Warden met me outside the lift.

He was older than I remembered. Or maybe just more tired. High-rank black jacket, no insignia except the pin—an open flame with a single eye at its center.

“Lieutenant,” he said, curt. “I was hoping I got a chance to meet you before the festivities. Let me just say what an honor it is to have you in our humble facility.”

His eyes didn’t blink. He was waiting to be seen.

“I need to see the prisoner,” I said.

He stiffened. Just a twitch. But I caught it.

“Intelligence asset,” I added. “She’s not just a rebel. She was command. If she talks—”

“Of course,” he cut in. “Anything you need. Your operation brought glory to the Signal. More than glory. It brought balance. Heresy fell silent that day.”

He gestured with two fingers. A young officer ran to hold the lift open.

I stepped inside. The Warden followed.

The doors whispered shut. The chamber lifted. No music. Just the hum of sterilized air.

He didn’t speak until we cleared the tenth floor.

“She?” he asked, softly. “You still refer to it that way?”

I didn’t answer.

The same tone. The same pause I’d heard from command.

“She has valuable information,” I said flatly. “And she surrendered under combat stress. I need to verify her condition before the tribunal.”

The Warden didn’t push back. Just nodded. A little too slow. Like he was calculating something.

When the lift slowed, he straightened his collar.

“You’ll find her intact,” he said. “Mostly. Just remember, sir—mercy here is defined by function.”

The doors opened.



####

The noise hit me before the door even opened.

Screams, mechanical and human, layered over each other like teeth grinding in rhythm. The whine of pumps. The static cough of loudspeakers. Babies crying—dozens, maybe hundreds—all out of sync, all wrong. Somewhere down the corridor, a man sobbed without shame. Somewhere else, metal slammed against flesh.

It was a symphony of suffering. A deliberate design. No one here was allowed to forget what pain sounded like.

And still, it wasn’t the volume that hollowed me out. It was the indifference. Like the tower itself had made peace with agony. Like it enjoyed it.

The Warden walked beside me into the cell.

A barred pit yawned in the center of the floor—maybe twenty feet deep. No lights. Just sound. The moaning, screaming, coughing chaos of bodies kept just below sight. I couldn’t see them. Didn’t need to. They were the warning.

She was huddled in the corner. Naked. Skin bruised and red where the restraints had dug in. Still breathing. Still alive. That shouldn’t have been possible. Then I saw why.

A guard was pissing on her.

I moved without thinking. Slapped him so hard he spun into the wall.

"What the fuck is this?" I barked.

The other two flinched. One reached for his blade.

Good. I hoped he did. I felt the grin before I knew it was on my face.

But the Warden raised a hand. “Stand down.”

The guard’s fingers hovered at the hilt. Then dropped.

“She’s not a she,” the Warden said. Nervous now. Like he’d just stepped off protocol. “The prisoner is classified male. Genetic designation confirmed.”

I stared at him. Looked at her, looked back at him.

“She’ll be punished as such. Scalping. Surgical redaction. Burned alive. Heretic tier. No Spark harvest. No reclamation. The most severe tier available.”

Even I gasped. I hadn’t known. Or maybe I had. And wouldn’t let myself know.

She stood up. Slowly. Blood on her thighs. Shoulders square. Penis soft but visible. Defiant.

“Hiya, Captain America,” she said. “How’s it hanging?”

I couldn’t look away. Couldn’t look at anything else.

I loved her. I knew that. Whatever was between her legs didn’t change it.

“I’m sorry,” I whispered. “I shouldn’t have brought you here. I didn’t know—”

She gave a small shrug. “While I appreciate that, Captain, it’s a little late now.”

The Warden narrowed his eyes. “What is she talking about? Why is she calling you that?”

I turned to the guard who’d gone for his sword.

“You ever use that thing in combat?” I asked. “Or do you just show it off to the whores on Fremont?”

He flushed. Jaw tight.

“Gentlemen,” the Warden said, too bright. “Let’s all calm down. We have a common enemy.”

I didn’t even glance at him. “If she had that sword,” I said, nodding to Kat, “she’d kill you with it. She’s one of the finest warriors I’ve ever seen. She and her people took down a full choir of WRAITHs in hours. You will treat her with respect. And dignity. Get her some clothes."

The Warden blinked. Then laughed. “You want us to get the tranny clothes?” he said. “That’s hilarious, Lieutenant.”

My sword was out before I knew it. One clean stroke across his neck.

He was still laughing when his head hit the floor.

The plump body stood a moment longer. Then dropped like wet linen.

“Wow, Captain America,” Kat said. “You went and fucked up now.”

The angry guard lunged. I stepped sideways, caught his wrist, snapped the joint, and buried my elbow in his throat. He fell, gurgling.

The last one turned to run. I flung my sword.

It hit square between his shoulders. He dropped without a sound.

The cell fell still again. Just breath and blood and gravity.

But not for long.

I stepped over the Warden’s body and keyed the lock.

Her restraints hissed open. She didn’t move at first—just looked up at me, blood streaked across her chest and cheek like warpaint.

I held out my hand.

She took it.

When she stood, her body shivered once. Then stilled. Her eyes met mine. No thanks. No fear.

“Bet you wish we’d have just fucked,” she said.

I looked away. My ears burned. “That’s not—”

“Relax, Captain. Just checking if you still blush.”

I opened my mouth. Closed it. Shook my head.

She stepped toward the rack where the guard had thrown his coat. Draped it over her shoulders. Didn’t bother with shame.

“While I appreciate the rescue,” she said, voice low, “why bring me here in the first place if this was how it was gonna end?”

I stared at the pit. The sounds still rising.

“I don’t know,” I said. “I thought… I don’t know what I thought.”

“So now what, Captain America?”

“I haven’t had a chance to work out the details,” I said. “But it involves a lot of fighting. Some killing. And then running.”

She laughed once. Dry. “Typical. No real plan. Just run around and break shit. Just like your kind.”

“SynRgy?” I asked.

“No,” she said. “Men.”

I didn’t argue.

“In all seriousness,” I told her, “you’re going to have to go on without me. Six floors down, there’s an exit point. I’ll hold them off as long as I can. But they can see through my eyes. Hear through my ears. Maybe even—”

“No,” she cut in. “They can’t hear your thoughts.”

I blinked. “You know about—?”

“Of course we know. Your spinal feed gives them data—motion, vitals, threat index. But not thoughts. Not yet.”

She stepped behind me.

“Hold still,” she said. “Fair warning—it’s going to hurt a lot worse on the way out than it did going in.”

Her fingers brushed the back of my neck. Then came the blade.

The pain wasn’t sharp—it was ancient. Like someone was carving out a lie I’d been telling since birth. I bit down hard enough to taste blood.

She dropped the wet device onto the floor. It sparked once. Then died.

Two more guards burst into the room.

No time to speak.

The floor was already slick with blood.

We moved together—her with a stolen blade, me with nothing but muscle and rage.

One guard went for his gun. She buried the sword in his throat.

The other swung wild. I caught his arm, shattered his shoulder, drove my knee into his ribs until he stopped moving.

We stood there.

Alive.

Together.

Who knew how long it would last.
